summaries.headline: Learning Drives AI Adoption Among Education Users
summaries.summary: Google's global survey of 21,000 people across 21 countries shows learning has overtaken curiosity as the primary reason people use AI tools, with students, teachers, and parents emerging as the most active users. Eighty-five percent of students aged 18 and over report using AI for schoolwork and complex topics, while 81 percent of teachers use AI tools compared to 66 percent of the general public. The shift from experimentation to everyday educational utility suggests AI has moved beyond early adopters to become a mainstream learning resource across diverse global markets.

summaries.headline: Brookings Study Finds AI Risks Now Outweigh Educational Benefits
summaries.summary: A Brookings Institution study drawing on input from 505 participants across 50 countries warns that generative AI's current use in education is putting students at risk, with harms now overshadowing benefits. The year-long "premortem" research found 56 percent of responses highlighted AI harms compared with 44 percent citing benefits, with particular concerns about overreliance undermining students' capacity to learn independently and develop social-emotional skills. The findings suggest that decisions made now about AI implementation will determine whether the technology strengthens foundational learning or erodes the conditions required for learning to occur.

summaries.headline: Oklahoma Pushes New Literacy Laws Without Clear Funding
summaries.summary: Oklahoma lawmakers are advancing new literacy legislation aimed at improving reading outcomes statewide, but educators are raising concerns about implementation costs. The proposed measures would likely require districts to adopt new curricula, provide additional teacher training, and expand reading intervention programs without dedicated state funding streams. District leaders warn that unfunded mandates could force schools to reallocate resources from other critical programs or leave rural and low-wealth communities unable to fully comply with new requirements.

summaries.headline: Delaware Task Force Proposes Major Funding Shift to High-Needs Students
summaries.summary: Delaware's Public Education Funding Commission is developing a hybrid formula that would direct significantly more money to low-income and multilingual learners while preserving elements of the state's 1940s-era unit-based system. The proposal could add $200 million to $400 million to the state's $2.3 billion education budget, with commission leaders pointing to higher-performing neighboring states that spend more per pupil and target funds to high-needs populations. The shift reflects growing recognition that Delaware's current approach leaves the state trailing regional peers on key academic outcomes, suggesting lawmakers may face pressure to approve substantial new investments in the coming budget cycle.

summaries.headline: California Awards $15M for Literacy Coach Training
summaries.summary: California will award $15 million to a county office of education or consortium to train educators as literacy coaches and reading specialists, with priority given to applicants partnering with higher education institutions that have demonstrated success in multilingual education. The single grant, authorized by Assembly Bill 121 and running through June 2029, specifically targets professional development for English learners and other high-need student groups through dual language immersion and multilingual programs. The substantial investment signals the state's recognition that specialized literacy expertise—particularly for multilingual learners—requires dedicated funding and systematic training beyond what districts can typically provide internally.

summaries.headline: Teachers Boost Literacy Using Students' Cultural Backgrounds
summaries.summary: Elementary educators who participated in a professional learning community focused on critical reading with multicultural texts reported improved outcomes in vocabulary, comprehension, and word recognition among their students. The Child Trends study found that content-based instruction reflecting students' personal histories, communities, and cultures consistently led to academic success in reading across multiple classroom settings. The approach offers districts a research-backed strategy for literacy improvement at a time when some educators face emerging curricular restrictions on discussing students' racial, ethnic, and cultural backgrounds.

summaries.headline: Chicago Public Schools Loses Market Share Despite Stable Demographics
summaries.summary: Chicago Public Schools' share of the city's school-aged children has dropped from roughly 75% to 71% since 2018, representing 18,000 fewer students according to a new analysis by Kids First Chicago. The district's "market share" had remained consistent through previous challenges including mass school closures, financial troubles, and strikes, but began slipping in recent years even as migrant student arrivals temporarily stabilized overall enrollment. The trend suggests families are increasingly choosing alternatives to CPS at the same time the district faces growing numbers of severely underenrolled schools and serves students with higher needs.

summaries.headline: Oaklyn District Adds Literacy Screeners, Sees Attendance Gains
summaries.summary: Oaklyn schools rolled out new early literacy tools this year, including Kickstart Literacy for 20-minute daily phonics instruction in preschool and GOLDfinch game-based assessments to track letter and sound knowledge. The district also implemented Al's Pals social-emotional curriculum using puppet shows to build coping skills across preschool and kindergarten, while kindergarten continues its second year with Creative Curriculum's project-based learning approach. The combined focus on structured literacy instruction and social-emotional supports reflects growing recognition that early academic and behavioral foundations must be built simultaneously to drive later success.

summaries.headline: Media Partnership Brings Books to Title I Students
summaries.summary: KRIS 6 News partnered with the Scripps Howard Fund to deliver free books directly to students at Gloria Hicks Elementary School in Corpus Christi, allowing each child to select five books to take home. The "If You Give a Child a Book" campaign targets Title I schools and early-grade readers in underserved communities, addressing the critical gap in home library access that affects reading development. The initiative represents a growing model of media-community partnerships that bypass traditional distribution channels to place literacy resources directly in students' hands.

summaries.headline: Tech Leaders Convene on Student Data Protection
summaries.summary: School technology leaders gathered in Neosho to focus on strengthening protections for student data, addressing growing concerns about privacy in educational technology environments. The meeting brought together district officials to discuss best practices and strategies for safeguarding sensitive student information as schools increasingly rely on digital platforms and tools. The regional collaboration signals heightened awareness among education leaders that data security requires coordinated effort beyond individual district boundaries.

summaries.headline: Two D.C. Schools Cut Chronic Absenteeism in Half
summaries.summary: Center City Congress Heights and D.C. Prep Anacostia have achieved the lowest chronic absenteeism rates of any schools east of the Anacostia River in Ward 8, where half of students district-wide are chronically absent. Both high-poverty schools saw their rates double during the pandemic before implementing sustained, trust-based strategies that brought attendance back to pre-pandemic levels. Their success stands out in a district where chronic absenteeism still hovers near 40 percent and research shows each missed day costs students one point on standardized assessments.

summaries.headline: Portland Mandates Attendance Teams After Safety Concerns Rise
summaries.summary: Portland Public Schools has mandated that all schools form data teams to examine attendance patterns and intervene with chronically absent students, after district research showed engagement and belonging drive attendance more than other factors. District officials report regular attendance has increased 2.3 percentage points to 74.24% as of December, with gains across all five focal student groups, though this year's student survey showed a spike in students citing safety concerns as a reason for skipping school. The district's systematic approach of combining data analysis with targeted interventions for students missing between 50% and 79% of school days demonstrates how attendance initiatives can yield measurable improvements when schools address root causes rather than symptoms.

summaries.headline: Language Platform Preply Raises $150M for Human-AI Tutoring
summaries.summary: Language learning platform Preply secured $150 million in Series D funding, reaching a $1.2 billion valuation as investors back models that combine human instruction with AI support. The company connects learners with tutors across more than 90 languages in 180 countries, using AI for lesson planning and progress tracking rather than replacing teachers, and reported becoming EBITDA positive within the past year. The funding reflects growing investor confidence in "human-led, AI-supported" education models as global demand for language learning reaches an estimated 1.8 billion people seeking second-language proficiency.

summaries.headline: Sparkli AI Raises $5M for Simulation-Based Learning
summaries.summary: Sparkli AI raised $5 million to replace text-based chatbots with interactive, simulation-driven learning experiences for students aged 5 to 12. The Zurich-based company, founded by Google veterans, is piloting its multimodal platform with over 100 schools and 100,000 students through one of the world's largest private school groups. The approach transforms passive AI interactions into active problem-solving environments where young learners build cities on Mars or engage in real-time debates rather than simply receiving lists of facts.

summaries.headline: Visual Literacy Emerges as Essential AI-Era Reading Skill
summaries.summary: Educators increasingly recognize visual literacy as a core reading strategy to help students navigate AI-generated content and combat misinformation in image-saturated learning environments. Students surrounded by photographs, diagrams, memes, and realistic AI-generated visuals need explicit instruction in reading images as constructed texts rather than neutral information, using the same critical thinking strategies applied to written texts. Schools that treat visual analysis as foundational reading instruction may better prepare students to evaluate and question the flood of AI-created content they encounter daily across academic and social contexts.

summaries.headline: Hawaii Joins 31 States Requiring Financial Literacy Courses
summaries.summary: Hawaii became the latest state to mandate financial literacy courses for high school graduation, with the requirement taking effect in the 2026-2027 school year and joining 31 other states with similar mandates. The move comes as national financial literacy rates remain stagnant at 49 percent since 2017, according to TIAA research, while only Alaska, Wyoming, and Washington D.C. currently have no financial literacy requirements. The expansion reflects growing state-level recognition that financial competency belongs in core graduation requirements, not just as an elective or integrated component.

summaries.headline: Community Volunteers Fill English Learning Gaps for Adults
summaries.summary: Literacy Volunteers of Charlottesville/Albemarle serves hundreds of adults annually with English skills and citizenship support, maintaining a persistent waiting list for tutoring services. The organization requires volunteers to commit two hours weekly with students, providing training and materials while seeking more tutors to reduce wait times for prospective learners. The demand-supply gap highlights how community-based adult literacy programs face capacity constraints even as immigrant and adult learner populations continue seeking English proficiency and civic integration support.

summaries.headline: AI-Powered Networks Cut Manual Maintenance for IT Teams
summaries.summary: School districts are deploying AI-native networking platforms to address chronic wireless outages and reduce the manual workload on stretched IT departments. Prosper ISD in Texas, serving 32,000 users with 10,000 more students expected, replaced failing controller architecture with Juniper Networks' Mist AI after experiencing daily wireless outages across multiple campuses. The AI systems automatically identify and resolve network issues with minimal human intervention, allowing three-click troubleshooting from district-wide views down to individual classroom access points.

summaries.headline: Oregon Lawmakers Propose Ending 27-Year School Funding Model
summaries.summary: Oregon legislators want to scrap the state's Quality Education Model and the commission that has guided school funding recommendations since 1999, replacing it with contracted research firms reporting to lawmakers every eight years. The proposal from Democratic lawmakers would eliminate the current system that targets a 90% graduation rate, though the Legislature has only fully funded schools to recommended levels once in the model's history — last year's record $11.4 billion allocation. The ambitious overhaul faces a tight timeline with just one month for debate during the upcoming legislative session, while lawmakers remain divided over both the timing and fundamental questions about what Oregon's public education system should achieve.

summaries.headline: Tanzania EdTech Fellowship Opens Third Cohort Applications
summaries.summary: Mastercard Foundation opened applications for its third Tanzania EdTech Fellowship cohort, targeting growth-stage education technology companies already demonstrating measurable impact in the region. The program offers up to $70,000 in equity-free funding, business mentorship, and regulatory guidance to help companies reach 8,000 to 10,000 learners, with applications closing January 31, 2026. The fellowship reflects sustained investment in locally-founded EdTech solutions as African education systems look to technology platforms for scalable access to quality learning, particularly for underserved populations.

synthesis.topic_title: Science of Reading Mandates Drive Teacher Certification Overhauls
synthesis.whats_happening: State education boards are mandating teacher certification alignment with science of reading principles, moving beyond voluntary professional development approaches. Federal agencies are simultaneously directing substantial funding toward evidence-based literacy interventions, with a particular focus on scalable tutoring models.
synthesis.whats_driving: Post-pandemic reading score declines have intensified pressure on states to implement research-backed literacy instruction methods. Federal grant priorities are shifting toward proven interventions that can demonstrate measurable impact at scale, while expanding definitions of literacy now include media and AI competencies alongside traditional reading skills.

- Federal literacy grants are targeting partnerships between state departments and research institutions for scalable interventions
- Literacy scope is expanding beyond traditional reading to encompass media literacy and AI literacy as core educational competencies
- Professional development models are pivoting from general literacy training to targeted, evidence-based methodologies

# IDOE, Notre Dame awarded $10M federal grant to scale literacy tutoring

[![Portrait of Rayleigh Deaton](https://www.southbendtribune.com/gcdn/authoring/authoring-images/2025/08/04/NSBT/85508957007-07312025-headshots-3.jpg?crop=5099,5098,x1146,y0&width=48&height=48&format=pjpg&auto=webp) Rayleigh Deaton](https://www.southbendtribune.com/staff/74952152007/rayleigh-deaton/)

South Bend Tribune

Jan. 20, 2026, 5:05 a.m. ET

[Share to Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/dialog/share?display=popup&app_id=504023893921590&href=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.southbendtribune.com%2Fstory%2Fnews%2Feducation%2F2026%2F01%2F20%2Fidoe-notre-dame-awarded-10m-federal-grant-to-scale-literacy-tutoring%2F88195761007%2F)[Share to Twitter](https://x.com/intent/post?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.southbendtribune.com%2Fstory%2Fnews%2Feducation%2F2026%2F01%2F20%2Fidoe-notre-dame-awarded-10m-federal-grant-to-scale-literacy-tutoring%2F88195761007%2F&text=IDOE%2C%20Notre%20Dame%20awarded%20%2410M%20federal%20grant%20to%20scale%20literacy%20tutoring&via=SBTribune)[Share by email](mailto:?subject=IDOE%2C%20Notre%20Dame%20awarded%20%2410M%20federal%20grant%20to%20scale%20literacy%20tutoring%20-%20from%20South%20Bend%20Tribune&body=IDOE%2C%20Notre%20Dame%20awarded%20%2410M%20federal%20grant%20to%20scale%20literacy%20tutoring%0A%0AThe%20grant%20is%20awarded%20to%20IDOE%2C%20Notre%20Dame%20and%20WestEd%2C%20and%20it%20will%20help%20reach%20Hoosier%20students%20in%20rural%20and%20underserved%20areas.%0A%0ACheck%20out%20this%20story%20on%20southbendtribune.com%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fwww.southbendtribune.com%2Fstory%2Fnews%2Feducation%2F2026%2F01%2F20%2Fidoe-notre-dame-awarded-10m-federal-grant-to-scale-literacy-tutoring%2F88195761007%2F)

![The Golden Dome on top of the University of Notre Dame's Main Administration Building is seen through the trees on Monday, May 12, 2025, in South Bend, Indiana.](https://www.southbendtribune.com/gcdn/authoring/authoring-images/2025/05/20/NSBT/83747887007-05122025-notre-dame-campus-3.jpg?width=660&height=441&fit=crop&format=pjpg&auto=webp)

AI-assisted summary

- The Indiana Department of Education has received a $10 million federal grant to expand a literacy tutoring program.
- This grant will scale a high-impact tutoring model developed by the University of Notre Dame, focusing on the science of reading.
- The program aims to reach more students, especially in rural areas, by training tutors, aides, and parents.

SOUTH BEND — The [Indiana Department of Education](https://www.in.gov/doe/) has been awarded $10 million to scale a high-impact tutoring model developed by researchers at the [University of Notre Dame](https://www.nd.edu/), reaching additional Hoosier students around the state.

The [U.S. Department of Education](http://www.ed.gov/) awarded IDOE the [Education Innovation and Research (EIR) grant](https://www.ed.gov/grants-and-programs/grants-special-populations/grants-economically-disadvantaged-students/education-innovation-and-research#awards) in partnership with Notre Dame and nonpartisan research, develpoment and service agency [WestEd](https://www.wested.org/). Indiana was one of 10 states to receive an EIR grant this cycle, according to a [press release from Notre Dame](https://news.nd.edu/news/notre-dame-part-of-federally-funded-literacy-grant-to-expand-access-to-high-impact-tutoring-in-indiana/), part of a national push to accelerate literacy achievement across the country.

"Central to our state's continued improvement in literacy is bringing everyone to the table to identify and deploy solutions," Indiana Secretary of Education Katie Jenner said in the press release. "This grant will help us accelerate student learning through targeted, high-impact tutoring, deepen our commitment to the science of reading and better support educators and students across all 92 counties. We are grateful for this opportunity and eager to get to work for Indiana students!"

The press release says the funding will be used to support children's literacy by increasing the number of people connected to schools who are trained in the science of reading, including tutors, aides and parents. According to [prior reporting from IndyStar](https://www.southbendtribune.com/story/news/education/2025/07/28/new-indiana-3rd-grade-iread-law-raises-parent-concerns-over-retention/84264183007/), the science of reading is a body of research that focuses on how brains learn to read, with a heavy emphasis on phonics and phonemic awareness, along with teaching fluency, vocabulary and comprehension.

Using the grant money, the press release says, IDOE will build on Notre Dame's [Tutor-ND](https://sites.google.com/nd.edu/tutornd/home) model to expand the Tutor Cognitive Science Connection Hub (Tutor-CogSci), a "capacity-building model integrating high-impact tutoring with workforce development." This will help reach additional students, especially those living in rural and underserved areas of the state, while also building the future educator workforce.

Tutor-ND has been used in [South Bend schools](https://www.sb.school/) since its pilot program in 2021-22, with nine SBCSC schools implementing the high-impact tutoring model this year. Recently, nonprofit organization [We Make Indiana](https://www.wemakeindiana.org/) held a [press conference](https://www.southbendtribune.com/story/news/education/2026/01/12/south-bend-schools-we-make-indiana-calling-for-volunteer-tutors/88083731007/) to call for 50 community members to receive training from Tutor-ND and become tutors in SBCSC schools.

"Coinciding with the rollout and expansion of the program, SBCSC’s IREAD pass rate rose from 53.7 percent in 2021 to 70.8 percent in 2025 — the largest gain among comparable public districts in the state," the Notre Dame press release says. "Those partnered with Tutor-ND increased IREAD pass by 26.4 percentage points, five times the state average over the same period. Work with Catholic schools resulted in increases to above 95 percent pass rates for all participating schools."

Notre Dame will serve as an "implementation and research-practice partnership hub" to provide support and infrastructure for the program, while WestEd will act as an independent evaluator to assess impact and support scalability.

# LearningMole Launches AI Literacy Initiative to Help Teachers and Parents Prepare Children for an AI-Enabled World

Distributed by EIN Presswire

_Educational platform announces expanded course programme addressing critical thinking, responsible use, and AI understanding for educators and families_

**AI is already part of children’s lives, whether we’re ready for it or not. The question isn’t whether children will use AI – it’s whether they’ll understand it well enough to use it wisely.”**

— Michelle Connolly, Founder LearningMole

BELFAST, COUNTY ANTRIM, UNITED KINGDOM, January 19, 2026 / [EINPresswire.com](https://www.einpresswire.com/)/ — LearningMole Launches AI Literacy Initiative to Help Teachers and Parents Prepare Children for an AI-Enabled World

Educational platform announces expanded course programme addressing critical thinking, responsible use, and AI understanding for educators and families

LearningMole, the free educational platform for children, has announced the launch of an AI literacy initiative aimed at equipping teachers and parents with the knowledge and tools to help children navigate an increasingly AI-influenced world.

The initiative builds on LearningMole’s existing AI for Education course for teachers and signals a commitment to developing further resources addressing one of the most pressing questions in modern education: how do we prepare children to use AI responsibly and think critically about the content it generates?

Speaking about the initiative, Michelle Connolly, Founder of LearningMole, said: “AI is already part of children’s lives, whether we’re ready for it or not. They’re encountering it in search engines, homework tools, games, and social media. The question isn’t whether children will use AI – it’s whether they’ll understand it well enough to use it wisely. We have a responsibility to teach critical thinking and ensure children see AI as a tool to support learning, not a replacement for it.”

Why AI Literacy Matters for Children

Artificial intelligence is no longer a future technology – it’s embedded in the tools children use daily. From voice assistants and predictive text to recommendation algorithms and AI-powered homework helpers, children are interacting with AI systems regularly, often without realising it.

This presents both opportunities and risks. AI can support learning, answer questions, and provide personalised educational experiences. But it can also generate inaccurate information, reinforce biases, and create a false sense of authority that children may not question.

The challenge for educators and parents is clear: children need to understand what AI is, how it works, and – most importantly – how to evaluate and question the outputs it produces.

“Children are naturally trusting,” said Connolly. “When a tool gives them an answer, they tend to believe it. That was fine when the tool was a dictionary or an encyclopaedia with editorial oversight. But AI can generate confident-sounding responses that are completely wrong. We need to teach children to ask questions: Where did this information come from? How do I check if it’s accurate? What might this tool have got wrong?”

AI literacy isn’t about making children fear technology or avoid using helpful tools. It’s about developing the critical thinking skills that allow them to benefit from AI while recognising its limitations. These skills will serve children throughout their education and into their future careers, where AI proficiency combined with human judgement will be increasingly valuable.

LearningMole’s AI for Education Course

LearningMole’s AI for Education course provides teachers with a practical introduction to AI and its applications in educational settings. The self-paced course covers what AI is, how teachers can use it effectively, and how to approach AI tools with appropriate understanding of both their capabilities and limitations.

The course is designed as a starter resource for educators who want to understand AI better before introducing it into their practice or discussing it with students. Available for £9.99, it provides an accessible entry point for teachers at any level of technical confidence.

“Many teachers tell us they feel overwhelmed by AI,” said Connolly. “There’s so much information, so many tools launching every week, and genuine uncertainty about what’s appropriate in educational settings. Our course cuts through the noise and gives teachers a solid foundation – what AI actually is, what it can realistically do, and how to think about using it responsibly.”

The course addresses practical questions teachers face: Which AI tools are appropriate for classroom use? How can AI support lesson planning and administration without compromising educational quality? What do teachers need to know to guide students who are already using AI for homework?

Expanding the Course Programme

Building on the success of the initial AI for Education course, LearningMole is developing an expanded programme of courses addressing AI literacy for both teachers and parents.

Planned courses will cover:

For Teachers:

Using AI for efficiency in lesson planning, resource creation, and administration

Introducing AI literacy concepts to primary and secondary students

Evaluating AI tools for educational appropriateness and accuracy

Developing classroom activities that build critical thinking about AI

Addressing AI use in homework and assessment

For Parents:

Understanding the AI tools children encounter daily

How to talk to children about AI at different ages

Supporting children to use AI responsibly at home

Recognising AI-generated content and teaching children to do the same

Balancing AI assistance with independent learning and thinking

“We’re committed to supporting both teachers and parents,” said Connolly. “Children don’t just encounter AI at school – they use it at home, on their devices, in their games. Parents need practical guidance on having these conversations and setting appropriate boundaries. Teachers need resources they can use in the classroom. We want to provide both.”

The expanded course programme will roll out over the coming months, with LearningMole seeking input from teachers and parents on the topics and formats that would be most useful.

A Practical Guide for Parents: Talking to Children About AI

Parents play a vital role in helping children develop healthy, critical relationships with AI technology. Here are practical approaches for different ages and situations:

Start with what they already know

Children are often using AI without realising it. Start conversations by exploring tools they’re familiar with – voice assistants, autocomplete on phones, recommendation systems on streaming services. Help them understand that these are all forms of AI making predictions and suggestions based on data.

Explain that AI learns from patterns, not understanding

One of the most important concepts for children to grasp is that AI doesn’t “understand” information the way humans do. It identifies patterns in data and generates responses based on those patterns. This means it can produce confident-sounding answers that are factually wrong because it’s predicting likely word combinations, not verifying truth.

Model critical evaluation

When using AI tools together, demonstrate questioning: “Let’s check if this is accurate.” “I wonder where this information came from.” “What might the AI have got wrong here?” Children learn critical thinking by watching adults practise it.

Discuss the difference between assistance and replacement

Help children understand that AI works best as an assistant, not a replacement for their own thinking and learning. Using AI to explain a concept they’re struggling with is different from using it to write their homework. The first builds understanding; the second bypasses it.

Set clear expectations about honesty

Children need to understand that passing off AI-generated work as their own is dishonest. Have clear conversations about what’s acceptable and what isn’t, and explain why – not just because it’s against school rules, but because it cheats them out of actual learning.

Stay curious together

AI is evolving rapidly, and none of us have all the answers. Approach it as something you’re learning about together. This models intellectual humility and shows children that questioning and learning are lifelong practices.

A Practical Guide for Teachers: Building AI Literacy in the Classroom

Teachers are on the front line of helping children develop critical thinking about AI. Here are practical approaches for introducing AI literacy:

Make AI visible

Many children use AI without recognising it. Create activities that help students identify AI in their daily lives – the recommendations on YouTube, the suggestions in search engines, the filters on social media. Building awareness is the first step to critical engagement.

Teach how AI works at an appropriate level

Children don’t need to understand machine learning algorithms, but they benefit from understanding basic concepts: AI learns from data, it identifies patterns, it makes predictions. Simple analogies can help – AI is like a very fast pattern-matcher that has read enormous amounts of text but doesn’t actually understand what any of it means.

Create opportunities to catch AI mistakes

One of the most powerful lessons is discovering that AI gets things wrong. Design activities where students fact-check AI outputs on topics they know well. When children catch AI making errors, they develop healthy scepticism that transfers to future use.

Discuss bias and limitation

AI systems reflect the data they’re trained on, including its biases and gaps. Age-appropriate discussions about where AI information comes from and what might be missing help children think more critically about all information sources, not just AI.

Model appropriate use

Show students how you use AI as a professional – for generating ideas, checking understanding, or saving time on administrative tasks – while demonstrating that you verify important information and don’t rely on AI for final judgements. Modelling good practice is more powerful than lecturing about it.

Develop clear policies and discuss the reasoning

Students respond better to rules they understand. Explain why certain AI uses are acceptable and others aren’t. Focus on learning outcomes: the goal of education is developing knowledge and skills, and anything that bypasses that process ultimately harms the learner.

Keep learning yourself

AI tools are evolving constantly. Commit to ongoing learning about new developments, and be honest with students when you’re uncertain. This models the lifelong learning mindset that will serve them well in a rapidly changing world.

Critical Thinking: The Core Skill

At the heart of AI literacy is critical thinking – a skill that extends far beyond technology. Children who learn to question AI outputs, verify information, and think carefully about sources are developing capabilities that will serve them across every area of life.

“The goal isn’t to make children suspicious of everything,” said Connolly. “It’s to help them develop the habit of thinking before accepting. That’s valuable whether they’re evaluating AI-generated content, reading news articles, hearing claims from friends, or making decisions about their own lives. AI literacy is really just critical thinking applied to a new and powerful technology.”

LearningMole’s approach emphasises that AI is a tool – potentially a very useful one – but never a replacement for human understanding, creativity, and judgement. Children who learn to use AI wisely will have advantages in education and careers. Children who learn to think critically will have advantages in everything.

The platform hosts over 3,500 English-language educational videos alongside growing collections in Spanish and Irish, covering subjects including maths, science, English, geography, history, and digital skills.

Founded by Michelle Connolly, a former primary school teacher, LearningMole is committed to making quality educational resources accessible to every child, regardless of school budgets or family circumstances. # Pa. Literacy Coalition applauds state Board of Education for major step to align teacher certification with the science of reading

![](https://cdn3.locable.com/uploads/resource/file/1270270/fit/800x600/Screenshot_202026-01-21_20at_201.41.04_E2_80_AFPM.png?timestamp=1769027326)

**The Pennsylvania Literacy Coalition commended the Pennsylvania State Board of Education for its decisive action during its 389th meeting, advancing recommendations that would strengthen teacher certification assessments by aligning them with evidence-based reading instruction grounded in the science of reading.**

At the meeting, the board’s Teacher and School Leader Effectiveness Committee recommended that the Secretary of Education consider whether a new or revised certification assessment aligned to the Pre-K–6 grade-band changes enacted under Act 47 of 2025 should require candidates to demonstrate knowledge of evidence-based reading practices. Notably, the committee emphasized that literacy competencies should be assessed across all relevant certificates—not limited to elementary pathways—and embedded directly in licensure testing wherever reading instruction is part of a teacher’s role.

“This is a huge and meaningful step forward for Pennsylvania,” said Rachael Garnick, Pennsylvania Coalition manager for Teach Plus, on behalf of the Pennsylvania Literacy Coalition. “By naming the science of reading as a required component of licensure assessments, the State Board is signaling that evidence-based instruction is not optional—it is foundational to teaching.”

The coalition, which is composed of more than 70 organizations from across the Commonwealth representing educators, school and district leaders, healthcare professionals, business leaders, parents, and advocates, noted that the board’s action closely aligns with recommendations it formally shared with the Board in 2025, which called for strengthening Pennsylvania’s teacher certification exam so it serves as a clear, rigorous measure of candidates’ readiness to teach reading using evidence-based practices. Those recommendations emphasized the critical role licensure assessments play in shaping educator preparation programs and ensuring new teachers enter classrooms prepared to meet the needs of all learners.

“As Pennsylvania continues this work, we are hopeful the final assessment selection process includes strong guardrails, such as stand-alone, evidence-based measures of reading competency that apply across all relevant certification areas,” Garnick added. “Getting licensure right is one of the most powerful levers the state has to ensure lasting, systemwide alignment with the Science of Reading.”

The Pennsylvania Literacy Coalition stands ready to support the State Board of Education and the Department of Education as this process moves forward and looks forward to continued partnership to strengthen teacher preparation and improve literacy outcomes for students across the Commonwealth.

# Opinion: UCLA must require media literacy as a general education class

![](https://wp.dailybruin.com/images/2026/01/IMG_1499.png)

(By Ella Han / Daily Bruin)

[![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/a54be0d50ba053fa11dd330461b2d223?s=512&d=mm&r=pg)](https://dailybruin.com/author/antonia-pinnar)

### By [Antonia Pinnar](https://dailybruin.com/author/antonia-pinnar)

#### Jan. 21, 2026 3:45 p.m.

UCLA claims its purpose is the “creation, dissemination, preservation and application of knowledge.”

Yet today’s students are consuming more information than any generation before them, without ever being formally taught how to evaluate it.

Information can spread rapidly, is available at our fingertips and holds the ability to shape our shared beliefs – especially in the digital age.

As such, it is essential for all people to learn [critical media literacy skills](https://dailybruin.com/2025/05/14/opinion-now-more-than-ever-we-must-combat-the-decline-of-media-literacy). In this pursuit, UCLA must make media literacy a general education requirement.

It has become [increasingly difficult to determine what information is fake or altered](https://dailybruin.com/2026/01/09/trump-mischaracterizes-undie-run-video-as-reaction-to-nicolas-maduro-capture) with the rapid development of artificial intelligence technology.

“I worry a lot about AI and being able to make stuff up online. (People) post whatever they want to and give a lot of misinformation,” said Rebecca Chang, a first-year ecology student. “That could lead to a lot of problems in people’s views.”

But the more common issue is the lack of recognition of biases in the media.

“One of the biggest challenges for evaluating information is judging bias,” said Jeff Share, a senior lecturer in the School of Education and Information Studies. “A lot of people focus on just authenticity and being able to tell if it’s real or if it’s fake, but I think that’s a simplistic idea that really doesn’t help us nearly as much as if we are questioning the bias, because bias always exists, whether we realize it or not.”

The danger lies not in the existence of bias, but in our lack of awareness of bias. Blindly accepting ideas without any critical thought can have dangerous consequences because the media holds the power to influence our perspectives, shape our ideologies and affect our actions.

For instance, a [study](https://sciences.ucf.edu/news/how-fake-news-affects-u-s-elections/) by the University of Central Florida found that during the 2016 election, the top 20 misinformed news stories received more engagement than the top 20 factual stories.

Students may disagree, believing that adding a GE requirement creates an unnecessary burden. Yet nowadays, media analysis is not an elective skill.

A [UNESCO study](https://www.unesco.org/sites/default/files/medias/fichiers/2023/11/unesco_ipsos_survey.pdf) found 85% of respondents are concerned about the impact of misinformation. Without being taught to be critical thinkers, students are vulnerable to misinformation, may unintentionally reinforce echo chambers and perpetuate harmful ideologies.

Even younger students recognize the importance of media literacy. According to the [News Literacy Project](https://newslit.org/news-and-research/news-literacy-in-america/), 94% of teens want media literacy to be taught in schools.

“It would just be beneficial overall, because it’s something that has been becoming more prominent in every field,” said Brigitte Chamu, a second-year economics student. “It’s important for every student, regardless of their major, to have this knowledge.”

Media literacy is a necessary skill across every academic field.

Share said that it is fascinating to see how students from disciplines ranging from pre-med to pre-law bring distinct perspectives to media analysis. He added that media literacy is universally relevant because even something as technical as a biology article is still media.

Critical media literacy courses may combine media interpretation and production, a method utilized in Share’s courses at the School of Information and Educational Studies. By including not only analysis, but also production, students are able to see what happens behind the scenes and just how easily bias influences messaging.

“(Students) being able to do their own research/being able to distinguish information for themselves, would be helpful,” Chamu said.

Even the state of California is taking steps to incorporate media literacy into education through the adoption of [Assembly Bill 873](https://legiscan.com/CA/text/AB873/id/2782355), which integrates this skill in K-12 schools. UCLA must aid in this effort. Developing critical media literacy skills is a pursuit that should not end in high school.

If UCLA intends to prepare students for a world shaped by misinformation and bias, then media literacy cannot be optional.

Ultimately, UCLA’s mission of “creation, dissemination, preservation and application of knowledge” means nothing if students are unaware of their civic responsibility to question such knowledge.

(John Smierciak/for the Post-Tribune)

![Author](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/?s=85&d=mm&r=g)

By [Carole Carlson](https://www.chicagotribune.com/author/carole-carlson/ "Posts by Carole Carlson") \| Post-Tribune

PUBLISHED: January 18, 2026 at 10:30 AM CST

**Getting your [Trinity Audio](https://trinityaudio.ai/) player ready...**

The Indiana Department of Education, in partnership with the University of Notre Dame, has received a $10 million federal Innovation and Research grant to improve student literacy.

Officials announced the grant at the Jan. 14 State Board of Education meeting.

They said Indiana was one of 10 states receiving the grant this year to improve literacy practices, especially in rural and underserved areas.

Officials said Indiana’s proposal focused on strengthening evidence-based literacy instruction aligned to the science of reading phonics-based approach.

Notre Dame’s model, called Tutor-ND, expands tutoring access by building capacity in schools and community organizations with its training.

“Central to our state’s continued improvement in literacy is bringing everyone to the table to identify and deploy solutions,” said Secretary of Education Katie Jenner.

A release said Notre Dame’s approach has produced strong early outcomes, especially in the science of reading priorities.

“The Tutor-ND program reflects the university’s long-standing commitment to expanding educational opportunities through data-driven practices and partnerships that strengthen communities,” said Notre Dame President Rev. Robert A. Dowd.

_Carole Carlson is a freelance reporter for the Post-Tribune._
